title: Why does Andy Burnham hate Holyrood?
gist: Prospect's David McAllister argues Andy Burnham's vague 'power balancing' devolution rhetoric treats Holyrood as an obstacle rather than a foundation, and warns that bypassing the Scottish parliament via Westminster (breaking the Sewel convention) would undermine the devolved settlement's legitimacy just as new survey data shows rising, not falling, Scottish support for Holyrood's authority.

insights.claim: Scottish government public service reform secretary Ivan McKee said of Andy Burnham's 'power balancing' devolution proposals that he has no idea what Burnham means and doubts Burnham knows himself, underscoring how undefined the agenda remains even to Holyrood insiders.
insights.speaker: David McAllister
insights.so_what: Signals that any devolution reform Burnham might pursue as a future PM contender is still undefined — too vague yet to price as a concrete policy risk or catalyst.
insights.quote: I have no idea what he means and I don’t think he knows what he means.
insights.locator: What Burnham exactly intends to do

insights.claim: UK government engagement with 'Beyond Holyrood' devolution to date has mostly been financial rather than political — via the City Deals programme and the EU-replacement Shared Prosperity Fund, which was rebranded and considerably defunded under Keir Starmer into the 'Local Growth' and 'Local Regeneration' funds.
insights.speaker: David McAllister
insights.so_what: Marks a trend of shrinking direct UK regional-funding commitments even as devolution rhetoric intensifies — relevant to tracking UK regional fiscal transfers.
insights.quote: rebranded and defunded considerably under Keir Starmer as the “Local Growth” and “Local Regeneration” funds
insights.locator: In government, this has largely manifested

themes.theme: The real case for — and risk of bypassing — local reform

insights.claim: Scotland's 1994 model of blanket unitary local authorities has left local government disempowered, particularly in the Highlands, which is home to the largest council area in the UK.
insights.speaker: David McAllister
insights.so_what: Identifies a genuine, non-partisan case for Scottish local-government reform that any future devolution push could credibly target.
insights.quote: particularly the Highlands, which is home to the largest council area in the UK

insights.claim: A 2022 poll commissioned by Gordon Brown's Our Scottish Future found 45% of Scots surveyed would back directly elected provosts (Scotland's equivalent of mayors), evidence of real voter appetite for the kind of local reform Holyrood has not delivered.
insights.speaker: David McAllister
insights.so_what: Quantifies latent demand for a Burnham-style directly-elected-mayor model in Scotland — a possible wedge issue in future devolution debates.
insights.locator: one poll from 2022 commissioned by Gordon Brown’s Our Scottish Future

insights.claim: Burnham could pursue local-government reform in Scotland (e.g., directly elected mayors for Scottish cities) by breaking the Sewel convention — the informal rule under which Westminster avoids legislating on matters devolved to Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland — and imposing it via Westminster rather than through Holyrood and Scottish Labour leader Anas Sarwar.
insights.speaker: David McAllister
insights.so_what: A Burnham premiership testing the Sewel convention would make devolved settlements look 'arbitrary and provisional,' a durable constitutional risk for anyone tracking UK devolution or regulatory divergence.
insights.quote: the informal memorandum under which the British parliament avoids legislating on issues devolved to Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland
insights.locator: Here is one not entirely inconceivable scenario

themes.theme: Scottish public opinion cuts against a Westminster override

insights.claim: The latest Scottish Social Attitudes Survey — the most authoritative gauge of devolution attitudes since 1999 — found Scots hold their lowest trust in the SNP administration since the survey began, with 53% saying they trust it 'only some of the time' or 'almost never'.
insights.speaker: David McAllister
insights.so_what: Falling SNP trust is a headline metric for anyone tracking Scottish politics or independence-referendum probability ahead of the next Holyrood election.
insights.quote: “only some of the time” or “almost never”
insights.locator: Scots now hold the lowest esteem

insights.claim: Despite record-low trust in the SNP government, 67% of Scots say the Scottish government ought to have the most influence over how Scotland is run versus just 16% for Westminster — an increase from when the same question was asked in 2023.
insights.speaker: David McAllister
insights.so_what: Low SNP approval is not translating into support for recentralizing power in Westminster — support for Holyrood's authority is actually rising, strengthening the institutional case against a Burnham-style Westminster override.
insights.quote: 67 per cent said that they believed the Scottish government ought to have the most influence over how Scotland is run, compared to just 16 per cent who said the same of Westminster.

insights.claim: Holyrood's legitimacy derives from decades of grassroots campaigning rather than from any single party (SNP or Labour), which is why public backing for its authority persists even as trust in its current SNP occupants falls.
insights.speaker: David McAllister
insights.so_what: Explains why top-down Westminster moves to sideline Holyrood face durable public resistance regardless of which party runs it.
insights.quote: Devolution does not belong to the SNP, to Labour or to any political party. It belongs to the ordinary people who fought for its existence
insights.locator: Scotland’s parliament did not appear out of thin air
